entry.title: Re: S4HPC - Clear Outgoing Payments: Journal Entry Paymen...
entry.content:
Hello @vhardzeyenka 

Thank you for your question regarding setting the Item Payment Block automatically during the manual clearing process in the Clear Outgoing Payments app.

Currently, there is no standard BAdI or in-app configuration available to automatically set the Item Payment Block when performing manual clearing.

  • You can manually maintain the payment block after posting, using apps like Manage Supplier Line Items.
  • BAdIs such as FINS_JOURNAL_ENTRY_POST or FINS_ACDOC_POSTING exist for journal entry extensibility, but these do not control payment block behaviour within manual clearing processes.
  • If automation is critical, you would need to explore a side-by-side solution using SAP BTP, although Public Cloud limits direct updates to posted supplier documents.

Reviewing your system’s Extensibility Catalogue to confirm the latest available BAdIs, as capabilities may evolve with new releases.

SAP help reference documentation
Extensibility Catalogue – SAP Help

With regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: How to Implement Dynamic XSLT or Groovy Transformatio...
entry.content:
1. Understanding the Problem
You are receiving messages that are not always the same format. Depending on the message content or headers, each message needs to be transformed in a specific way. You want this to happen inside one integration flow without creating separate flows for every message type.

2. Deciding Transformations at Runtime
This is possible by using message headers or properties to carry values like "DocumentType". You can then use logic in the integration flow to check this value and decide which transformation to apply. The decision is made during runtime, based on actual message data.

3. Organizing Logic into Reusable Scripts
Instead of writing one large script for all types of messages, break the logic into smaller, reusable scripts. Each script handles one type of transformation and can be stored in a script collection. This makes your integration easier to read, test, and maintain.

4. CPI Limitations on Dynamic Loading
SAP CPI does not allow you to load scripts dynamically from external locations during runtime. It also blocks features like running raw code strings for security reasons. All transformation logic must be uploaded and packaged when you design and deploy the integration.

5. Using Message Properties for Routing
You can use a content modifier step to extract values from the message and store them in headers or properties. Later in the flow, you can use a router or a logic step to check this value and control which transformation is applied. This gives you flexibility and dynamic behavior based on message content.

6. Clean Design with Routers and Sub-Flows
For better readability, you can split the flow into multiple branches using routers. Each branch can handle a specific message type and run its own script or sub-flow. This avoids cluttering the main flow and keeps each transformation isolated.

7. Recommended Integration Patterns
Two useful patterns for this kind of use case are the dispatcher pattern and the router-plus-sub-flow pattern. The dispatcher pattern uses one logic step to choose the transformation, while the router pattern sends messages down different branches based on type. Choose the one that fits your team’s style and the complexity of your project.

8. What to Avoid for Maintainability
Avoid placing all transformation logic into one large script. Avoid using unsupported methods like dynamic script loading or code evaluation. Avoid mixing too many tools in a single flow unless necessary. Keeping logic modular and clean will save time in the long run.

9. Final Thoughts and Next Steps
Your goal of building a scalable and dynamic integration is achievable with good design. Use headers to carry decision-making data, organize scripts into manageable units, and route messages intelligently. If needed, you can sketch this out using a flow diagram or even create a base template for future integrations.

entry.title: Re: How to Approve/Reject Task via API in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
You can call this API:

https://<your SPA tenant URL>/workflow/rest/v1/task-instances?workflowInstanceId=<workflow instance ID>

You must authenticate using authorization code or password grant type, not client credentials, because you must have admin rights.

The call returns a list of tasks for that workflow, including ID:

entry.title: Re: 🚀 What’s New in Cloud Control Center PL21 – Smarter, Safer, and More in Control
entry.content:
Hi @SachinBG 

Thanks a lot for your comment and for sharing your experience.

The upgrade from Cloud Control Center PL20 to PL21 is designed to be straightforward, and i recommend following the process outlined in the official Administrator Guide Cloud Control Center Administrator Guide 

As for compatibility with SAP Business One, the verified combinations of B1 and Cloud Control Center versions are maintained in SAP Note 1756002. While this note lists the versions officially tested by SAP, please note that earlier 10.0 versions of SAP Business One also work with PL21, although they are not part of the formal test matrix.

Good luck with the upgrade!
Kind Regards
Guy

entry.title: Re: Automatic clearing of Incoming payments with Structur...
entry.content:
Hello,

Can you please check whether the following information is helpful?

Automatic Clearing of Bank Statement Items in S/4HANA Cloud

To address the issue of automatic clearing of multiple incoming payments within one line item in the CAMT.053 format, you can follow these steps:

1. Understanding Structured Remittance Information

The structured remittance information in the CAMT.053 format should ideally allow for automatic clearing of items. The reference number provided in the bank statement matches the invoice reference, which is crucial for clearing.

2. Configuration and Interpretation Algorithms

  • Interpretation Algorithm: You need to configure the interpretation algorithm correctly to handle multiple references in one line item. Algorithms like 1, 26, 421, and 28 can be tested, but they may fail due to specific conditions like leading zeros or special characters.
  • Algorithm 26: This can be used when there are leading zeros in the assignment number.
  • Algorithm 421: Useful when special characters are involved.

3. Handling Multiple Transactions

The system can automatically clear multiple incoming payments within one line item if the references are correctly interpreted. However, if all references are included in the memo line, it might require custom configuration or manual intervention.

4. Configuration Steps

  • Self Service Configuration UI (SSCUI): Automatic Supplier Payment Clearing in S/4HANA Pub… - SAP Community Use SSCUI to map external transaction codes to posting rules and determine the algorithm for automatic clearing.
  • Reprocessing Rules: Create reprocessing rules How to Create a Reprocessing Rule for One-Step Pos… - SAP Community to handle items that are not automatically cleared.

5. Common Practices

Grouping many incoming payments together is common in the CAMT.053 format. Ensure that your system is configured to handle such scenarios by adjusting the interpretation algorithms and reprocessing rules.

[1] Automatic Supplier Payment Clearing in S/4HANA Pub... - SAP Community
[2] How to Create a Reprocessing Rule for One-Step Pos... - SAP Community
Kind regards,
Henrike

entry.title: Another Year at SAP Inside Track Barcelona: My Experience
entry.content:
November arrived, and just like someone marking their favorite series on the calendar, attending SAP Inside Track Barcelona became an unmissable event. Eight years and counting! What started as a simple gathering has evolved into a tradition where the global SAP community connects to exchange knowledge, fresh ideas, and engage in discussions fueled by shared passion.

This year, I marked the date in bold and with an asterisk to ensure I was there from the very first coffee (that one which tastes even better when shared with long-lost friends).

The SIT Barcelona is not merely an event; it’s that time of the year when we learn from the best, rekindle old friendships, and speculate on the future of SAP. Without further ado, here’s an overview of this edition and the sessions that made us reflect, laugh, and even reconsider our strategies. Let’s dive in!

SITBarcelona Opening

We kicked off the event with David Ruiz welcoming us once again to the event.

@AAncos - Antonio de Ancos Cid | SAP 2024: From A to Z

If there was one session marked with a red circle in my agenda, it was Antonio de Ancos' session. Why? First, because of my admiration for such a great professional, and second, for the way he shares his knowledge on social media. Anyone following him knows that he always manages to keep us updated with the latest SAP news, all with his unique touch that makes learning a pleasure. And the best part? All in Spanish, of course! An inspiration for many of us!

This time was no exception: Antonio not only shared his knowledge but invited us to actively participate in his presentation with a fun icebreaker game from the very start. The game? A SAP version of Pasapalabra, where each question led us to explore concepts, trends, and novelties within the ecosystem. It was a very fun time where, amid laughter and answers (some more accurate than others), we went through new features, curiosities, and technical details. A unique way to learn and connect with the community!

Antonio Leite | Knowledge Graphs with SAP Data

Antonio introduced us to the fascinating world of Knowledge Graphs. He explained how a Knowledge Graph represents knowledge through nodes (entities) and edges (relationships), resembling the function of our own brains. Antonio didn’t stop at just the theory. He described a four-step process for building a Knowledge Graph within SAP and presented concrete examples of its use in solutions like SAP Integration Advisor and SAP Intelligent Situation Automation. In short, he demonstrated how SAP is integrating Knowledge Graphs into its ecosystem to create a smarter and more connected environment.

@Josetxu  - Josetxu Fernández | Gen AI in SAP Data and Analytics

Josetxu explained how SAP is integrating AI into its analytical tools, especially in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). They developed Just Ask, a new SAC feature that allows users to make queries in natural language. Instead of building complex queries with technical terms, users can simply ask questions like "show me the order value by product." Just Ask uses large language models (LLMs) to understand user intent and generate the corresponding query, also suggesting the most appropriate visualization for the data. A video showed practical examples of how to generate visualizations, filter data, and create low-code dashboards using Just Ask.

The architecture of Just Ask was also described, emphasizing that SAP’s LLM model is trained with internal data and does not use customer information, ensuring privacy. The evolution of Just Ask was presented, including future integration with SAP Data Sphere, support for new languages, and collaboration with Joule for a more complete user experience. This collaboration with Joule will enable easier dashboard creation with a low-code approach, the ability to generate scripts to customize dashboard behavior (e.g., creating buttons with specific actions), and the option to interact with data conversationally, like a chatbot.

This initiative aims to make data analysis accessible to a broader audience, regardless of their technical expertise.

Fran Villegas | Get Business Insights from Your SAP Data with Natural Language and Develop Gen AI Solutions Integrating AWS and SAP BTP Services

In this session led by Fran Villegas – Iberia Senior SAP Specialist SA at AWS – he provided a brief introduction to the relationship between AWS services and SAP, before discussing AWS’s Gen AI services and how they can be used with SAP. He also demonstrated integrations, including with SAP BTP.

The relationship between AWS and SAP began in 2008, when SAP started using AWS for its initial developments; it was the first certified cloud platform for SAP infrastructure. Early adopters migrated systems to the cloud, resulting in automation solutions from AWS partners to implement SAP services.

He introduced AWS Bedrock, a key tool that connects SAP to advanced language models via APIs. Notably, it allows customization with customer-specific data, ensuring privacy by keeping information within AWS accounts. The Bedrock-SAP integration enhances functionalities like natural language SQL generation and data-driven decision-making based on structured HANA Cloud data. Furthermore, services like QuickSight improve analysis.

The event demonstrations provided a practical approach to exploring the integration capabilities between AWS and SAP BTP. One of the workshops focused on developing a chatbot using AWS Bedrock, integrating data from SAP HANA Cloud. In this context, Bedrock facilitated the generation of SQL commands based on natural language. A demo on data flows included extracting data from SAP to QuickSight for analysis. These workshops also highlighted the flexibility in training AI models using SageMaker.

Coffee Break

Just when the much-awaited first break arrived to recharge with coffee and a sandwich or croissant, Lluís took the stage to surprise us. He showed the SIT Barcelona logo, a representation of the city's skyline, which led to an entertaining activity.

On X, they launched a challenge: guess as many monuments from the skyline as possible. Of course, I couldn’t resist participating, and although I was just one step away from guessing them all, luck was on my side! After revealing the logo and announcing the winner, Lluís shared the great news: I had won the monument contest! That said, the first monument in the skyline remains a mystery… Was it a pixel poorly cut? An abstract work of art? Who knows.

The important part is that I won my first prize of the day. A big thank you for that fun moment and the prize!

Alejandro Rodriguez Barea | SAP BTP Chef: The First SAP Demo Where All BTP Pillars Are Shown in a Practical Business Case

Alejandro focused on an innovative demonstration by the SAP Solution Advisory BTP team in Spain, where they showcased an app called BTP Chef, highlighting all the capabilities of SAP BTP working together.

The BTP Chef app integrates the five pillars of SAP BTP. Here’s a brief explanation of each pillar and how the app uses them:

  • Application Development: Both vendors and customers interact with the app through interfaces created with SAP Build Apps, enabling no-code user interface creation.
  • Automation: The workflow, from when the customer places an order to when it’s received, is managed with SAP Build Process Automation.
  • Integration: SAP Integration Suite connects different applications and systems, including non-SAP data systems, by exposing APIs and applying security policies, ensuring smooth and secure data flow.
  • Data and Analytics: SAP Analytics Cloud is used to monitor daily operations, creating a dashboard with key insights on orders, available ingredients, and other relevant metrics for decision-making.
  • Artificial Intelligence: Customer feedback analysis is carried out using the generative AI hub, accessible via SAP AI Launchpad and AI Core. The system categorizes feedback, suggests personalized responses, and even generates concise summaries to manage customer experience effectively.

The session emphasized that BTP isn’t just a collection of independent tools but a cohesive platform where each component complements others to create integrated solutions. It was definitely one of the sessions I recommend if you get the chance to see it.

Felipe Borges | Global eCompliance with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

In this session, a general overview of electronic compliance, electronic invoicing, and the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) solution was provided, focusing on combating tax evasion. It was highlighted that many countries, especially in Europe, are adopting an authorization model where transactions are reported to tax authorities in real time. This model, supported by solutions like SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), aims to close the tax evasion gap by validating electronic invoices at the time of the transaction. SAP’s DRC solution allows extracting data from SAP S/4HANA, transforming it into the required format for each country, and sending it to the corresponding tax authority. The session also included specific examples from Germany and Italy, countries that have implemented or are in the process of implementing mandatory electronic invoicing.

@tse-gdo - Gregory Doukas | Technician Training Tool Powered by Apple Vision Pro and SAP S/4 HANA

Once again, Gregory Doukas amazed us with his technical solution for an aviation company, combining SAP BTP with Apple Vision Pro. This solution leverages the power of Apple Vision Pro to simulate interaction with a real-scale airplane engine. Technicians can move around the virtual engine, using tools and following step-by-step instructions to complete maintenance tasks. The application monitors their progress, tracks the time spent, and certifies that the training has been completed. On the SAP side, a side-by-side extension application of SAP CAP on SAP BTP manages training data and compliance. The architecture also uses HANA Cloud for data storage and event-driven SDI data replication to keep information synchronized, with a RAG model and GPT-based AI interaction. This innovative solution not only enhances training efficiency but also reduces the risks and costs associated with using physical machinery for practice. Additionally, the immersive experience and AI assistance help technicians better understand procedures and acquire the necessary skills to perform their jobs with greater precision and safety. Looking forward to seeing what new innovations he will present next year.

Maria Codinach | Harsh Kumar | Frit Ravich’s Success Case in the Modernization of the Sales Application: From SMP to In-house Middleware

This session focused on the modernization of Frit Ravich’s Frit Sales application. The company transitioned from using SAP’s mobile platform, which had scalability and performance limitations, to a custom middleware solution based on Spring Boot. This change allowed Frit Ravich to handle large volumes of data and transactions, improving user experience and sales team efficiency. Integration with SAP ERP was maintained through a new gateway, ensuring synchronization of key data like orders, products, and customers. The new architecture, supported by tools such as Grafana and Nagios, offers greater flexibility, scalability, and real-time monitoring.

Lunch Break

During the lunch break at SAP Inside Track Barcelona, we had the opportunity to catch up with old colleagues, friends, and professionals from the SAP world. It was one of those moments where conversations flow between bites: we shared anecdotes, discussed the talks we had attended, how they fit into the current SAP landscape, and even ventured predictions about what the future holds for this ever-evolving ecosystem. It was a real pleasure to chat with such great professionals. There was even time for some laughs!

Niranjan Gattupalli | Implementing gCTS for Continuous Delivery in Complex SAP S/4HANA Landscapes: Branching Models, Hotfix Strategies, and 5-Tier Architecture

Niranjan’s talk focused on gCTS, a tool that combines SAP’s traditional change transport system with Git version control. gCTS offers advantages such as version control, branching, and merging code, facilitating the management of parallel versions and continuous delivery. However, it also presents challenges, such as limitations in working with feature branches and a lack of native protection against version degradation. Despite these challenges, gCTS offers greater flexibility in change management, especially in environments with multiple systems. Niranjan proposed a model for environments with limited resources, where a single system manages maintenance versions and urgent fixes, highlighting the importance of collaboration in overcoming gCTS implementation challenges.

Antonio Valladares Fernandez | Integration Suite Addon Built in SAP CAP by Code10

CI Accelerator, presented by Antonio Valladares, is a suite of tools designed to optimize and simplify integration projects in SAP Integration Suite. It combines methodologies, an add-on with additional functionalities, and intuitive applications to accelerate cloud adoption, ensure the quality and governance of integrations, and enhance the user experience.

The applications, developed with SAP CAP and UI5, allow seamless integration management, value mapping, and certifications. CI Accelerator also integrates with SAP's pipeline concept, facilitating the creation and management of complex workflows in a structured manner. In summary, CI Accelerator is a comprehensive solution that drives efficiency and satisfaction in integration projects with SAP Integration Suite.

@Bernhard - Bernhard Luecke | Reinventing Support with AI

Bernhard Luecke, the Global Head of Support Sales & Procure at SAP, shared insights on how SAP is leveraging artificial intelligence (AI) to transform customer support. With an impressive two million cases managed annually, AI emerges as a key tool to optimize efficiency and enhance the experience for both users and the support team.

One of the most notable aspects is using AI to provide intelligent recommendations for KBAs and notes. SAP is developing a system that utilizes AI to recommend the most appropriate communication channel based on the urgency and complexity of the issue. For cloud products, SAP plans to integrate direct access to support within the applications, making the support experience more seamless and intuitive. The ultimate goal is to use AI to predict issues and notify customers before they occur. This would involve analyzing customers' cloud systems, identifying potential risks, and offering preventive solutions to avoid impacting users.

Before kicking off the anticipated CodeJam led by Antonio Maradiaga, SAP Inside Track Barcelona gave us one of those exciting moments (with a bit of nervousness): the raffles! Books and a €100 SAP store gift card were up for grabs. As usual, the classic raffle tool was used, where names disappear one by one until a winner remains. The first draw started, and, as the anxious person I am, I searched for my name among the survivors. There it was! As fewer names remained, the adrenaline was rising: would I be the one? And yes, the last name standing was mine. My second prize of the day! It was epic. Then, with a big smile, we kicked off the CodeJam.

@ajmaradiaga  - Antonio Maradiaga | CODEJAM | SAP Build Code – Generative AI-Powered Development with Joule Copilot (including SAP Build Code and SAP Build Apps)

This year’s CodeJam was an epic experience! Led by Antonio Maradiaga, whom I had the pleasure of finally meeting in person (a true inspiration and the one responsible for motivating me to sign up for Devtoberfest!), the CodeJam took us through SAP Build Code with Joule AI, demonstrating how this tool powers AI-based development. The scenario simulated a customer loyalty program, integrating data on purchases, redemptions, and products from SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

This CodeJam not only showcased the power of low-code/no-code in a SAP environment but also demonstrated how artificial intelligence can accelerate the creation of business solutions. It was a moment of learning, creativity, and fun, highlighting the impact of these technologies on the future of development.

To wrap up, I want to say that it was an unforgettable day, where I was able to reconnect with old colleagues, meet new ones, and, of course, share insights with great professionals. I also want to thank the impeccable organization. Each year they manage to outdo themselves, and this time was no exception. My sincere thanks to @david_ruizb , @Lluis , @enric101 , Imma, Mireia, and   for their effort and dedication. See you next year; I’m already counting the days for the next edition!

I'm attaching the YouTube links for the sessions of SAP Inside Track Barcelona 2024:

  • Part 1 - SIT Barcelona 2024 online - 22 nov 2024 - 8.40 to 11.00 (CET)
  • Part 2 - Sit Barcelona 2024 online - 22 nov - 11.30 to 13.30 (CET)
  • Part 3 - SIT Barcelona 2024 online - 22 nov 2024 - 14:30 to 16.30 (CET)

Version en Castellano.

Noviembre llegó y, como quien marca en el calendario su serie favorita, la cita con el SAP Inside Track Barcelona era ineludible. ¡Ocho años y contando! Este evento, más que un simple encuentro, se ha convertido en una tradición donde la comunidad SAP, tanto local como global, se da un baño de conocimientos, ideas frescas y esas charlas que solo nacen entre apasionados por el mismo tema.

Este año no me lo pensé dos veces: marqué la fecha con negrita y asterisco para estar allí desde el primer café (ese que sabe mejor cuando lo compartes con amigos que hace tiempo no veías).

El SIT Barcelona no es solo un evento; es ese rincón del año donde aprendemos de los mejores, revivimos viejas amistades y, por supuesto, especulamos sobre lo que se avecina en el universo SAP. Así que, sin más preámbulos, te cuento cómo se vivió esta edición y las sesiones que nos hicieron reflexionar, reír y hasta replantearnos nuestras estrategias. ¡Allá vamos!

SITBarcelona Opening

Empezamos el evento con  David Ruiz dándonos la bienvenida un año más al evento. 

Antonio de Ancos Cid | SAP 2024: de la A a la Z

Si hay una sesión que estaba marcada en mi agenda con un círculo rojo, esa era la de Antonio de Ancos. ¿Por qué? Primero de todo por la admiración ante un gran profesional, y segundo por como comparte su conocimiento en redes sociales, quien lo sigue en redes sabe que siempre encuentra la forma de mantenernos actualizados con las últimas novedades del mundo SAP, todo con ese toque único que hace que aprender sea un placer. ¿Y lo mejor? Todo en español, claro. ¡una fuente de inspiración para muchos de nosotros!.

Esta vez no fue la excepción: Antonio no solo compartió su conocimiento, sino que nos invitó a ser parte activa de su presentación con un juego que rompió el hielo desde el minuto uno. ¿El elegido? Un Pasapalabra versión SAP, donde cada pregunta nos llevaba a explorar conceptos, tendencias y novedades del ecosistema. Fue un rato divertidísimo donde, entre risas y respuestas (algunas más acertadas que otras), recorrimos novedades, curiosidades y detalles técnicos. ¡Una forma única de aprender y conectar con la comunidad.!

Antonio Leite | Knowledge Graphs with SAP Data

Nos introduce al fascinante mundo del Knowledge Graph. Antonio nos explica cómo el Knowledge Graph representa el conocimiento a través de nodos (entidades) y aristas (relaciones), asemejándose al funcionamiento de nuestro propio cerebro. Antonio no se queda solo en la teoría. Describio un proceso de cuatro pasos para construir un Knowledge Graph dentro de SAP y presento ejemplos concretos de su uso en soluciones como SAP Integration Advisor y SAP Intelligent Situation Automation. En resumen, nos muestra cómo SAP está integrando el Knowledge Graph en su ecosistema para crear un entorno más inteligente y conectado.

Josetxu Fernández | Gen AI in SAP Data and Analytics

Josetxu nos explicó cómo SAP está integrando la IA en sus herramientas analíticas, especialmente en SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). Para eso crearon Just Ask, una nueva funcionalidad de SAC que permite a los usuarios realizar consultas en lenguaje natural. En lugar de tener que construir consultas complejas con nombres técnicos, los usuarios pueden simplemente formular preguntas como "muéstrame el valor de los pedidos por producto". Just Ask utiliza modelos de lenguaje extensos (LLM) para comprender la intención del usuario y generar la consulta correspondiente, además de sugerir la visualización más adecuada para los datos. Se muestra un video con ejemplos prácticos de cómo generar visualizaciones, filtrar datos y crear dashboards con low-code utilizando Just Ask.

Se describe la arquitectura de Just Ask, haciendo hincapié en que el modelo de lenguaje (LLM) de SAP se entrena con datos internos y no utiliza información de los clientes, garantizando la privacidad de los mismos. Se describe la evolución de Just Ask, incluyendo la futura integración con SAP Data Sphere, el soporte para nuevos idiomas y la unión con Joule para una experiencia de usuario más completa. En esta unión con Joule se conseguirá, creación de dashboards de forma más sencilla, utilizando un enfoque de low-code, el poder generar scripts para personalizar el comportamiento de los dashboards, por ejemplo, para crear botones con acciones específicas y por último, interactuar con los datos de forma conversacional, similar a un chatbot.

Con esto se busca hacer que el análisis de datos sea accesible a un público más amplio, sin importar su nivel de experiencia técnica.

Fran Villegas | Get business insights from your SAP data with Natural Language and Develop Gen AI solutions integrating AWS and SAP BTP Services

En esta sesión dirigida por Fran Villegas - Iberia Senior SAP Specialist SA en AWS, Nos hace una pequeña introducción sobre la relación de los servicios de AWS con SAP, para despues hablarnos de los servicios de Gen AI de AWS y como los podemos utilizar con SAP y nos enseñó unas demostraciones, una con una integración con SAP BTP.

La pequeña introducción de la relación de AWS con SAP nos habla de que la relación comenzó en 2008, cuando SAP empezó a utilizar AWS para sus desarrollos iniciales; fue la primera plataforma en la nube certificada para la infraestructura de SAP. Los primeros en adoptarla migraron los sistemas a la nube, lo que dio lugar a soluciones de automatización de los socios de AWS para implementar los servicios de SAP.

Nos presentó AWS Bedrock, una herramienta clave que conecta SAP con modelos de lenguaje avanzados mediante API. Destaca por permitir personalización con datos específicos del cliente, asegurando privacidad al mantener la información en cuentas de AWS. La integración Bedrock-SAP potencia funciones como generación de SQL en lenguaje natural y decisiones basadas en datos estructurados de HANA Cloud. Además, servicios como QuickSight mejoran el análisis.

La parte de las demostraciones mostradas en el evento proporcionaron un enfoque práctico para explorar las capacidades de integración entre AWS y SAP BTP. Uno de los talleres destacó el desarrollo de un chatbot utilizando AWS Bedrock, integrando datos de SAP HANA Cloud. En este contexto, Bedrock facilitó la generación de comandos SQL basados en lenguaje natural. Además, se presentó una demo de flujo de datos que incluyó extracción de datos desde SAP hacia QuickSight para análisis. Estos talleres también enfatizaron la flexibilidad en el entrenamiento de modelos de IA usando SageMaker.

Coffee Break.

Justo cuando llegó el esperado primer parón para recargar energías con un café y algún que otro bocata o cruasán, apareció en el escenario Lluís para sorprendernos. Mostró el logo del SIT Barcelona, una representación del skyline de la ciudad que dio pie a una dinámica bastante entretenida.

En X lanzaron un desafío: adivinar la mayor cantidad de monumentos del skyline. Por supuesto, no pude resistirme a participar y, aunque me quedé a un paso de acertar todos, ¡la suerte estuvo de mi lado! Después de revelar el logo y anunciar al ganador, Lluís me dio la gran noticia: ¡había ganado el concurso de los monumentos! Eso sí, el primer monumento del skyline sigue siendo un misterio… ¿Un pixel mal recortado? ¿Una obra de arte abstracta? Quién sabe.

Lo importante es que me llevé mi primer premio del día. ¡Muchísimas gracias por ese momento tan divertido y por el premio!

Alejandro Rodriguez Barea | SAP BTP Chef: first SAP demo where all the pillars of BTP are shown in a practical business case.

Se centró en una demostración innovadora realizada por el equipo de SAP Solution advisory BTP de España, donde nos mostró una aplicación llamada BTP Chef para mostrar todas las capacidades de SAP BTP trabajando juntas.

La aplicación BTP Chef integra los cinco pilares de SAP BTP, aquí dejo una breve explicación de cada pilar y de cómo los utiliza la aplicación:

  • Application Development: Tanto los proveedores como los clientes interactúan con la aplicación a través de interfaces creadas con SAP Build Apps. Lo que permite la creación de interfaces de usuario sin código.
  • Automation: El flujo de trabajo, desde que el cliente realiza un pedido hasta que lo recibe, se gestiona con SAP Build Process Automation.
  • Integration: SAP Integration Suite conecta las diferentes aplicaciones y sistemas, incluido un sistema de datos no SAP, exponiendo API y aplicando políticas de seguridad. Lo que garantiza un flujo de datos fluido y seguro.
  • Data and Analytics: Para monitorizar las operaciones diarias del chef, se utiliza SAP Analytics Cloud para construir un panel de control. Este panel proporciona información clave sobre los pedidos, los ingredientes disponibles y otras métricas relevantes para la toma de decisiones.
  • Artificial Intelligence: El análisis de los comentarios de los clientes se realiza utilizando el centro de IA generativa, accesible a través de SAP AI Launchpad y AI Core. El sistema es capaz de categorizar los comentarios, sugerir respuestas personalizadas e incluso generar resúmenes concisos para facilitar la gestión de la experiencia del cliente

La charla enfatiza que BTP no se trata solo de un conjunto de herramientas independientes, sino de una plataforma cohesiva donde cada componente se complementa para crear soluciones integrales. Para mi es una de las sesiones que recomiendo si podéis ver.

Felipe Borges | Global eCompliance with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

En esta sesión se proporciona descripción general del cumplimiento electrónico, la facturación electrónica y la solución de SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), y como combatir la evasión fiscal. Se destacó que muchos países, especialmente en Europa, están adoptando un modelo de autorización donde las transacciones se reportan a las autoridades fiscales en tiempo real. Este modelo, apoyado por soluciones como SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), busca cerrar la brecha de la evasión fiscal al validar las facturas electrónicas en el momento de la transacción. La solución DRC de SAP permite extraer datos de SAP S/4 HANA, transformarlos al formato requerido por cada país y enviarlos a la autoridad fiscal correspondiente. La charla también mencionó ejemplos específicos de Alemania e Italia, países que han implementado o están en proceso de implementar la facturación electrónica obligatoria

Gregory Doukas | Technician Training Tool powered by Apple Vision Pro and SAP S/4 HANA

Un año más Gregory Doukas nos sorprendió con su solución técnica para una empresa de aviación que combinaba SAP BTP y las Apple Vision Pro. El caso aprovecha la potencia de Apple Vision Pro para simular la interacción con un motor de avión a escala real. Los técnicos pueden moverse alrededor del motor virtual, utilizando herramientas y siguiendo instrucciones paso a paso para completar las tareas de mantenimiento. La aplicación monitoriza su progreso, el tiempo que tardan y, finalmente, certifica que han completado el entrenamiento. En el lado de SAP, una aplicación de extensión lado a lado de SAP CAP en SAP BTP gestiona los datos de capacitación y el cumplimiento. La arquitectura también utiliza HANA Cloud para el almacenamiento de datos y la replicación de datos SDI impulsada por eventos para mantener la información sincronizada y un modelo RAG  con chat GPT para la interacción de IA. Esta solución innovadora no solo mejora la eficiencia de la capacitación, sino que también reduce los riesgos y los costes asociados al uso de maquinaria física para la práctica. Además, la experiencia inmersiva y la asistencia de la IA ayudan a los técnicos a comprender mejor los procedimientos y a adquirir las habilidades necesarias para realizar su trabajo con mayor precisión y seguridad. Con ganas del ver que novedad nos presenta el año que viene.

Maria Codinach | Harsh Kumar | Frit Ravich's success case in the modernization of the sales application: from SMP to in-house middleware

La charla se enfocó en la modernización de la aplicación Frit Sales de la empresa Frit Ravich's. Se pasó de utilizar la plataforma móvil de SAP, que presentaba limitaciones de escalabilidad y rendimiento, a un middleware propio basado en Spring Boot. Este cambio permitió a Fre-Rabic manejar un gran volumen de datos y transacciones, mejorando la experiencia del usuario y la eficiencia del equipo de ventas. La integración con el sistema SAP ERP se mantuvo a través de un nuevo gateway, asegurando la sincronización de datos clave como pedidos, productos y clientes. La nueva arquitectura, apoyada por herramientas como Grafana y Nagios, proporciona una mayor flexibilidad, escalabilidad y monitorización en tiempo real.

Lunch Break

Durante el descanso para comer en el SAP Inside Track de Barcelona, tuvimos la oportunidad de compartir un rato genial con antiguos compañeros, amigos y profesionales del mundo SAP. Fue ese tipo de momento en el que, entre un bocado y otro, las conversaciones fluyen: compartimos anécdotas, discutimos las charlas que habíamos presenciado y cómo encajan en el panorama actual de SAP, y hasta nos atrevimos a hacer predicciones sobre lo que nos espera en el futuro de este ecosistema que no deja de evolucionar. Un verdadero placer compartir charlas con grandes profesionales. ¡Hasta hubo tiempo para unas risas!

Niranjan Gattupalli | Implementing gCTS for Continuous Delivery in Complex SAP S/4HANA Landscapes: Branching Models, Hotfix Strategies, and 5-Tier Architecture

La charla de Nanjan se centra en gCTS, una herramienta que combina el sistema tradicional de transporte de cambios de SAP con el control de versiones de Git. gCTS ofrece ventajas como el control de versiones, la ramificación y la fusión de código, facilitando la gestión de versiones paralelas y la entrega continua. Sin embargo, también presenta desafíos como la limitación en el trabajo con ramas de funciones y la falta de protección nativa contra la degradación de versiones. A pesar de estos desafíos, gCTS permite una mayor flexibilidad en la gestión de cambios, especialmente en entornos con múltiples sistemas. Nanjan propone un modelo para entornos con recursos limitados, donde un único sistema gestiona versiones de mantenimiento y correcciones urgentes, y destaca la importancia de la colaboración para superar los retos de la implementación de gCTS.

Antonio Valladares Fernandez | Integration Suite addon build in SAP CAP by Code10

CI Accelerator, presentado por Antonio Vades, es una suite de herramientas diseñada para optimizar y simplificar los proyectos de integración en SAP Integration Suite. Combina metodologías, un add-on con funcionalidades adicionales y aplicaciones intuitivas para acelerar la adopción de la nube, garantizar la calidad y gobernanza de las integraciones, y mejorar la experiencia del usuario.

Las aplicaciones, desarrolladas con SAP CAP y UI5, permiten una gestión fluida de integraciones, mapeos de valores y certificados. Además, CI Accelerator se integra con el concepto de pipeline de SAP, facilitando la creación y gestión de flujos complejos de manera estructurada. En resumen, CI Accelerator es una solución integral que impulsa la eficiencia y la satisfacción en los proyectos de integración con SAP Integration Suite.

Bernhard Luecke | Reinventing Support with AI.

Bernard Luecke que es el Global Head of Support Sales & Procure at SAP nos habló sobre cómo SAP está aprovechando la inteligencia artificial (IA) para transformar la manera en que se brinda soporte a los clientes. Ante la impresionante cantidad de dos millones de casos que SAP gestiona anualmente, la IA emerge como una herramienta clave para optimizar la eficiencia y la experiencia tanto para los usuarios como para el equipo de soporte.

Uno de los aspectos más destacables es el uso de la IA para proporcionar recomendaciones inteligentes de KBAs y notas a los usuarios. SAP está trabajando en un sistema que utiliza la IA para recomendar el canal de comunicación más adecuado según la urgencia y complejidad del problema. Para los productos en la nube, SAP planea integrar el acceso al soporte directamente en las aplicaciones. De esta manera, el usuario podrá acceder a la ayuda sin tener que salir de la aplicación en la que está trabajando, haciendo que la experiencia de soporte sea mucho más fluida e intuitiva. El objetivo final de SAP es utilizar la IA para predecir problemas y notificar a los clientes antes de que estos se produzcan. Esto implicaría analizar los sistemas de los clientes en la nube y detectar posibles riesgos, ofreciendo soluciones preventivas para evitar que el problema llegue a afectar al usuario.

Antes de arrancar con el esperado CodeJam liderado por Antonio Maradiaga, el SAP Inside Track Barcelona nos regaló uno de esos momentos llenos de emoción (y un poco de nervios): ¡los sorteos! Libros y una tarjeta de 100 euros en la tienda SAP estaban en juego. Como es tradición, se usó la mítica herramienta de sorteos del SIT, donde los nombres desaparecen uno a uno hasta quedar un ganador.

El primer sorteo empezó y, como buen ansioso, busqué mi nombre entre los sobrevivientes. ¡Ahí estaba! Cada vez quedábamos menos, y la adrenalina subía: ¿seré yo? Y sí, el último nombre en pie fue el mío. ¡Mi segundo premio del día! Fue épico. Luego, con la sonrisa bien puesta, arrancamos el CodeJam.

Antonio Maradiaga | CODEJAM | SAP Build Code – Generative AI-powered development with Joule copilot (include SAP Build Code and SAP Build Apps)

¡El CodeJam de este año fue toda una experiencia épica! Liderado por Antonio Maradiaga, a quien por fin tuve el gusto de conocer en persona (¡un crack y también el culpable y un referente que me inspiró a inscribirme en el Devtoberfest!), El CodeJam nos llevó a explorar SAP Build Code junto con Joule AI, mostrando cómo esta herramienta potencia el desarrollo basado en inteligencia artificial. El escenario propuesto simulaba un programa de fidelización de clientes, integrando datos relacionados con compras, canjes y productos desde SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Este codejam no solo mostró el poder del low-code/no-code en un entorno SAP, sino que demostró cómo la inteligencia artificial puede acelerar la creación de soluciones empresariales. Fue un momento de aprendizaje, creatividad y diversión, dejando claro el impacto de estas tecnologías en el futuro del desarrollo.

 

Para finalizar quería decir que fue un día inolvidable, donde puede volver a rencontrarme con antiguos compañeros, poder conocer de nuevos y como no, compartir opiniones con grandes profesionales. Además, quiero dar las gracias a la impecable organización. Cada año logran superarse, y esta vez no fue la excepción. Mis agradecimientos más sinceros a David, Lluis, Enric, Imma, Mireia y Losel por su esfuerzo y dedicación. ¡Nos vemos el próximo año, ya estoy contando los días para la próxima edición!

entry.title: Re: 依据生产订单预留创建领料申请
entry.content:
Machine translation:

Create Withdrawal Request from Production Order Reservation
Business Context:
Based on the customer’s business, a large production order is maintained for one month for a product, so for the raw material demand in the production order, there is only one month’s total demand and quantity, but in case of production withdrawals, the production department fills the withdrawal requirement with the quantity actually withdrawn to the warehouse and the warehouse personnel issue the quantity of the withdrawal request directly to the production order based on the withdrawal request maintained by the production department
Business Needs:
The production department can create a material withdrawal reservation (request) directly from production order component maintenance, the quantity of the withdrawal request is consistent with the actual quantity withdrawn to the warehouse, and the warehouse can post directly from the withdrawal request created by the production department

entry.title: Re: 生产订单联产品结算规则必填
entry.content:
Machine translation:

Production order co-product settlement rule is mandatory
Business Context:
In the normal scenario, the co-products of the product will yield, but due to temporary adjustments to the production process or changes in the product characteristics, the co-products originally set up do not yield
System Functional Restrictions:
If there is a production order for co-products for which there is a product that has already been put away, it cannot be deleted from the production order components even if the co-product has not been put away, and the settlement rule for the co-product in the production order, which is a mandatory field, cannot be set to blank, so if the product is not produced, but because of the control of the system function, the settlement rule for co-products also needs to be maintained and ultimately has an impact on cost apportionment

entry.title: Re: Issue Importing Customer Master Data with Multiple Co...
entry.content:
Hi,

In addition to my previous comment:

I think the key here is if this scenario is needed for planning or analytics only ( or both).

If analytics only :

Having them all separate dimensions without properties is fine because the link between all those dimensions will come from the transactional data and along with cascading effect, your reports will just be fine.

If planning as well:

If you model all customer , sales org, distribution channel, division as separate dimensions, you’d likely need some validation logic to keep them in sync — otherwise users will need to select all of them separately in planning templates which could create a poor / disconnected user experience. Keeping them as properties and defining validation rules avoid this issue ( validation limits apply ).

Also, adding too many dimensions can make the model harder to maintain / design for planning. The SAC modelling checklist suggests keeping models to around 8–12 dimensions when possible – in reality it is usually difficult to keep this limit with clients having complex data model / planning requirements. Therefore, it might be worth checking / challenging if this level of granularity is really required. For example why would Company Code alone is not enough  or is there a strong business need for planning at the Sales Org level within the same company code? If it is really required, having them as separate dimensions, but also properties along with validation rules could be a solid option.

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Barcelona 2025 - Nov 14th
entry.content:
Hi Barcelona and SAP Community !!

We are very excited to announce to you that SAP Inside Track Barcelona 9th edition will take place next November 14th 2025

A SAP Inside Track (SIT) is a local grassroots community organised event where SAP Community Network members come together to share knowledge and expertise and to network about SAP related topics. SAP Inside Tracks have been very successful in countries all over the World.

Come to learn and share your knowledge with your peers as well as make new friends in an informal atmosphere.

SAP Inside Track events offer SAP Community provided sessions from SAP Experts and passionate SAP Community members.

We invite you to participate and help us to do a great event.  Don’t forget to invite your colleagues.

Where : Modernist Room (Carrer de Roger de Flor 48, 08018 Barcelona)  https://goo.gl/maps/Kp25w8mZgLo

Who : For local and global SAP community (customers, consultants, freelancers, startups, press and bloggers, SAP employees) plus for those totally new to SAP.  The most important is that you want to share and gain some knowledge and new buddies.

Registration : WIP. Stay tuned for updates.

Sessions and Agenda :  This year 2025, agenda (*) will have next schema

  • Opening SITBarcelona
  • SITBarcelona sessions (Block 1)
  • Break/beverages & snacks
  • AUSIA-AUSAPE : SAP BTP Hackathon Final
  • Lunch
  • SITBarcelona sessions (Block 2)
  • Closing SITBarcelona

* A detailed agenda will be published around midOctober.

(NEW) Call for speakers and content is OPEN  :   LINK to summit your proposals

(NEW) You will find SITBarcelona event here :

https://community.sap.com/t5/barcelona-events/sap-insisde-track-barcelona-2025/ec-p/14145343#M8

Blusky : Official account will be @sitbarcelona.bsky.social‬

Twitter : Official account will be @sitbarcelona

Hashtags for SAP Inside Track Barcelona are  #SITBarcelona and #SITBCN

About complete agenda, sponsorship packages, accomodation and travel info, social event,etc.. please contact us or take a look at SIT Barcelona site https://sitbarcelona.com

Hope to meet all of you there!

Lluis Salvador Suarez, Enric Castella , Imma Salmeron,  Mireia Bubí ,

Losel Tenzin ,  Saul Quintero,and David Ruiz Badia

entry.title: Adjusting FI imported data in GR Doc Type 01
entry.content:
Hi Experts,
I’d like to hear others’ experience or advice re post year adjustments & the integration of FI and GR.

Scenario as follows:
Closed the period in FI 12.24
Performed the UJ release for period 12.24
Recorded additional adjustments to the financials in GR with doc type 01 in 12.24 (Doc type is configured same as Best Practice: Posting level 01 not reversing – current or subsequent year).
Ran Balance carry forward (BCF) in GR.
During 2025 we bring in each period - FI via UJ release bringing in the movements.

In 2025 the adjustments posted in 12.24 with DT01 were updated in FI. So now by importing the FI balances we are double counting and will result in an out of sync balance between FI and GR.
How is this scenario handled?
I proposed making these types of adjustments in GR that will be eventually recorded and imported from FI via a PL10 reversing Doc type. This will avoid double counting and maintain the balance synchronization.
Any ideas or thoughts on this?

Using SAP S/4HANA 2023 On premise that is converted from EC-CS (ECC previously upgraded to S/4H).

Regards,
Eyal

entry.title: Re: Posting failed: Unable to post using posting rule F02...
entry.content:
Hi HY,

 

for such reasons there is an application log. You can open it by navigating to the Reprocess Bank Statement Item App and open that item which is not completed. Click on Log and have a look there why your rule was not applied. Could be different reasons, hard to tell without any further information. But most importantly is, this is how you should approach such a problem in the future. Always have a look on the application log yourself to understand why the system did what it did.

 

Best,

Edrilan Berisha

entry.title: Sap project system integration with production order
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

We are currently using project system with WBS for accounting and control of projects costs, so we are using just WBS elements, we plan costs and resources outside SAP.

We would like to plan materials and labour also in SAP for some small production processes, so we are setting up networks and activities for planning costs, scheduling dates and monitoring through progress analysis/EVM and so on.

Our concern is that production department is already planning and recording actual costs like labours (through confirmations) and materials at production order level with WBS element as account assignment.

So our concern is how to best model the production order related planning inside SAP PS using networks and activities.

What would you suggest as a solution in this case? Also what would it be your suggestion in case of a greenfield implementation?

Thanks for helping,

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud: Secure by Design and Intelligent by Default
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor and Compliance and has been recognized as a Fellow of Information Privacy (FIP) by the International Association of Privacy Professionals (IAPP). As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as cybersecurity, data privacy, secure data flow, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit, and compliance.)

Introduction

In today’s fast-moving business world, data is everywhere—but often stuck in silos, difficult to manage, and even harder to turn into action. SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) helps solve that. It is a fully managed (SaaS) cloud solution that brings together data from both SAP and non-SAP sources, making it easier to unify, govern, and use data across your business. For organizations already using SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW), BDC offers a clear path to modernize in the cloud. At the core of BDC are Data Products—reusable, trusted datasets enriched with business context—and Intelligent Applications—AI-driven apps that consume these products to automate decisions and actions. By combining tools like SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud into a unified architecture, BDC removes the need for complex data extractions and ensures your data is always analytics ready.

In this blog, we will explore how BDC works, its key building blocks, and the strong security framework that keeps your enterprise data safe and actionable to enable secure by design and intelligent by default.

Core Components of SAP Business Data Cloud

SAP BDC is built upon a robust stack of integrated components, each playing a vital role in its functionality:

  • SAP Datasphere: The bedrock of SAP BDC, Datasphere provides a unified environment for comprehensive data integration, modeling, transformation, and governance. It supports diverse storage options—including high-performance in-memory, cost-effective disk, and object storage—via SAP HANA Cloud and Data Lake. It includes the Unified Semantic Layer, which standardizes business definitions and supports zero-copy data access across SAP and non-SAP tools. This flexibility makes it ideal for managing varied data needs and enables the creation and governance of reusable, business-ready data products.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC): Seamlessly integrated with Datasphere, SAC delivers advanced analytics, planning, and predictive capabilities. It provides real-time dashboards, embedded AI/ML insights, and dynamic visualizations that drive both operational efficiency and intelligent decision-making.
  • SAP Databricks: An SAP-managed Databricks runtime natively embedded within BDC, it brings powerful AI/ML, data science, and big data engineering capabilities directly into the SAP ecosystem. Utilizing zero-copy Delta Sharing, it directly accesses governed data products from SAP Datasphere, enabling advanced analytics on both SAP and non-SAP data without the need for replication.
  • The BDC Cockpit: Serving as the central administrative console for SAP Business Data Cloud, the BDC Cockpit allows users to manage roles, configurations, and deployments. For data analysts and data scientists, it also acts as the primary entry point to discover, activate, and monitor data products and intelligent applications.

 Figure 1: SAP Business Data Cloud - Core Components

 

Category

Component/Description

Key Functionality/Characteristics

SOURCE SYSTEMS

SAP S/4HANA (Cloud)

Origin of transactional and operational data

 

SAP SuccessFactors

Origin of HR data

 

SAP Cloud ERP

Origin of enterprise resource planning data

 

SAP BW/4HANA

Origin of historical and analytical data

 

Third-party systems

Origin of external data from non-SAP sources

SAP DATASPHERE

Connectors to source systems (e.g., SDA, DP Agent, JDBC, SDI)

Facilitates data ingestion from diverse sources

(DATA FABRIC LAYER)

Semantic modeling, data lineage, transformation

Defines data meaning, tracks data flow, prepares data for consumption

 

SAP Foundation Services: harmonization + metadata

Ensures data consistency and provides descriptive information about data

 

SAP Databricks runtime for zero-copy access & ML ops

Enables advanced data processing, machine learning operations directly on data without copying

 

Storage in SAP HANA Cloud or Data Lake

Stores integrated and processed data

DATA PRODUCTS LAYER

Curated & governed datasets

Provides ready-to-use, validated, and controlled data assets

 

Based on harmonized views and tables from Datasphere

Data products derived from the structured and harmonized data in Datasphere

 

Include metadata, access policies, lineage

Ensures data discoverability, security, and traceability

INTELLIGENT APPLICATIONS

SAP/Partner-built AI-driven business apps

Applications leveraging AI for specific business functions, built by SAP or partners

 

Use Data Products as input (not raw data)

Consume refined and governed data, not direct source data

 

Automate insights, simulate, forecast

Core capabilities of intelligent applications for business decision support

 

Embedded ML (Joule copilot, time-series models, simulations)

Machine learning capabilities integrated directly within applications, enhancing functionality

BROADER ECOSYSTEM

SAP Analytics (Cloud)

Tools for data visualization, reporting, and business intelligence

 

Joule

AI assistant/copilot for intelligent interactions (likely related to embedded ML)

 

SAP Databricks (used within Datasphere)

Platform for ML workflows & services, analytical queries, direct access to data products

 

GenAI Hub

Centralized hub for Generative AI capabilities and models

With a clear understanding of the components, let’s explore how key architectural principles—like data gravity and zero-copy sharing—bring these components to life. The principle of data gravity dictates processing data where it resides, minimizing unnecessary movement and duplication. This approach dramatically enhances performance, ensures data consistency, and simplifies governance. Building on this, zero-copy sharing allows datasets to be securely shared across different internal services and external platforms, such as SAP Databricks, without physical replication. This innovative capability ensures data freshness, eliminates redundancy, and drastically reduces the operational overhead associated with traditional data duplication.

Principle

Meaning

How SAP BDC Implements It

Data Gravity

Data attracts compute and services to where it resides, minimizing unnecessary movement.

Replicates source data into Datasphere once; downstream consumers access it in place.

Zero-Copy Data Sharing

Share data across services and tools without physically copying it.

Uses Delta Sharing to provide real-time access to data without duplication.

Governance by Design

Embed data quality, access control, and monitoring into the data lifecycle.

Implements RBAC, metadata, certification, and lineage across data products.

Semantic Consistency

Ensure consistent business definitions and semantics across all systems.

Applies Unified Semantic Layer for standard definitions across SAP and non-SAP data.

Open Data Consumption

Expose data through standardized APIs and protocols to support diverse tools.

Supports APIs, JDBC/ODBC, Delta Sharing, and open metadata standards (ORD, CSN).

AI-First Enablement

Design the architecture to natively support AI and machine learning workloads.

Provides curated data products for AI; integrates with SAP Joule and Databricks ML.

Federated but Governed Access

Enable distributed access while maintaining centralized visibility and control.

Formation-based access, central cockpit governance, and role-specific permissions.

Data-as-a-Product

Treat data as reusable, governed assets with clear ownership and lifecycle.

Data Products are cataloged, versioned, governed, and optionally monetized.

Multi-Layer Security in SAP BDC:

Customer Data Isolation:

The diagram above outlines the multi-layered isolation and security model underpinning SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). Each layer—from tenant and application isolation to data access and encryption—is designed to ensure secure separation of customer environments and data. BDC leverages SAP BTP subaccounts, SAP Analytics Cloud spaces, and HANA Cloud schema-level isolation to enforce strong boundaries across structured and unstructured data. Federated identity via SAP IAS and XSUAA, combined with fine-grained RBAC and AppRouter security, ensures only authorized users and apps can access data. Critically, all operational access is just-in-time, logged, and monitored, providing a strong audit trail and compliance posture. This layered defense approach enables secure, scalable, and compliant operations across diverse enterprise landscapes.

 Figure 2: Secure Multi-Layer Isolation

Security in SAP Business Data Cloud is enforced across every architectural layer, from ingestion to consumption. The table below highlights specific security considerations for each core component—covering access controls, identity management, encryption, data governance, and metadata protection. It reflects how BDC applies fine-grained controls using SAP IAS, RBAC, and semantic governance, while supporting zero-copy access and tenant isolation to ensure secure, scalable operations.

Component

Security Considerations

SAP and Non-SAP Source Systems

Source system authentication and authorization managed externally. Integration to BDC via secure connectors like ODP, JDBC, SDI DP Agent, with TLS encryption.

SAP BTP Customer Sub-Account

Tenant-level isolation. Access managed through SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS), role-based access control (RBAC), and audit logging.

SAP Datasphere Instance

Provider-managed, provisioned within customer sub-account. Encrypted data at rest and in transit. Includes data lineage, access control, metadata protection.

SAP Data Products

Granular access control (RBAC), visibility settings (public, private, internal). Metadata includes data lineage, classification, ownership for governance.

SAP Datasphere Semantic Layer

Access control per semantic model. Inherits governance from data products. Metadata visibility is controlled.

SAP Databricks

Zero-copy access with authentication via shared identity provider. Data access governed via semantic layer permissions. Execution isolated in managed environments.

Intelligent Applications

Formation-based access controls. Deployed in provider-managed secure spaces (read-only). Can be copied into customer-managed spaces with RBAC.

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)

User authentication via IAS or SAP Universal ID. Live data connections use secure tunnels. Dashboard access controlled via SAC roles and teams.

Business Users/App UI

User-level permissions defined via roles. Interaction restricted by app layer controls, enforced least privilege access.

Data Scientist

Access to data products governed through RBAC. Execution environments are containerized and isolated. Notebook access secured via SAP-managed identity.

Data Products and Intelligent Applications

Data Product

  1. Standardized, curated, and governed datasets enriched with business context, semantics, and metadata.
  2. Designed for high-volume reuse across analytics, AI, and ML scenarios—reducing the need for custom pipelines or redundant extraction.
  3. Can be developed by SAP, customers, or partners.
  4. Stored flexibly across SAP Datasphere and SAP HANA Cloud Data Lake, supporting zero-copy access for downstream tools like SAP Databricks.
  5. Easily discoverable and installable through the BDC Cockpit and Data Marketplace, which provide rich metadata and role-based access control.
  6. Typically installed into customer-managed SAP Datasphere spaces, allowing customization while preserving governance.

Intelligent Application

  1. Prebuilt, AI-infused apps developed by SAP and partners to automate and optimize business workflows.
  2. Use Data Products as inputs for planning, simulation, forecasting, and decision support.
  3. Deploy with supporting assets like SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards, semantic models, and predefined logic.
  4. Designed for business users to interact with real-time insights securely and intuitively—no data wrangling required.

 Figure 3: Data Products and Intelligent Application

 

Security Domain

Data Products Layer

Intelligent Applications

Access Control

RBAC, Authentication via IAS, Delta Sharing policies

Formation-based access, user assignment controls app/data scope

Metadata & Lineage

ORD & CSN Standards, Data Product Certification

Transparent logic, knowledge graphs, semantic awareness, based on trusted data

Encryption

At Rest: SAP KMS; In Transit: TLS 1.2+

Same encryption as Data Products

Identity & Access

2FA via IAS/Universal ID

Inherits identity context, JSON Token, Scope, Role Collection; 2FA enforced via login

Responsibility Model

SAP: Infra & Platform Security

Customer: Roles, Identity Policies, Visibility

Conclusion

SAP Business Data Cloud is a secure and AI-ready platform that helps businesses manage and use complex data from across their systems. Built on technologies like SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, and SAP Databricks, it ensures data is high-quality, governed, and well-protected. At its core are Data Products—reusable, trusted datasets that follow a “data-as-a-product” model. These products are secured, certified by SAP, and governed through centralized policies.

Layered on top are Intelligent Applications, which use these data products to automate decisions across business processes. With support for both SAP and non-SAP data, and powered by advanced AI tools, these apps deliver fast, accurate insights to drive better outcomes.

Disclaimer:

© 2025 SAP SE or an SAP affiliate company. All rights reserved. See Legal Notice on www.sap.com/legal-notice for use terms, disclaimers, disclosures, or restrictions related to SAP Materials for general audiences.

entry.title: Find the Bug 🐞 Formatting dates and times in Build Apps
entry.content:
Someone sent me a problem they were having in a private message, that made me chuckle.

To be honest, after 2 years of war and 2 weeks of intense war, and having to live out of a suitcase for a week in Athens because my plane was diverted and could not enter my country -- this was a fun, amusing diversion (it actually made me laugh).

Here's the message I received:

FORMAT_DATETIME_WITH_TIMEZONE(DATETIME("2025-04-03T02:00:00.000Z"), "MMM d, yyyy", "America/Chicago")

"I am using formula to format the date. Actually the output should be Apr 2, 2025, 9 pm. But I am getting Apr 3, 2025, 9 pm. How to solve this? I tried multiple ways. It's only changing time but not date. I used other timezone format as well. Please help. This is an issue in production."

After running the formula in my Build Apps and getting the same result, I assumed it was a bug in the tool (@Mari and @Pekka_Aaltonen, please forgive me). For the programmer, the obvious thing to think was that you asked for the date in the format April 2 (because it had to give the day before because the time zone they gave was minus 6 hours from UTC) and instead it was giving April 3. Why? Probably because the formula was too stupid to return the previous day's date.

But, alas, there was a perfectly logical explanation, but it was hidden because of the actual date and time used in the example. Another case of all-too-human error.

Can you figure the bug out?

P.S.: If you like this puzzle, wait for Devtoberfest in October. We'll have plenty more.

entry.title: Re: Adjusting FI imported data in GR Doc Type 01
entry.content:
As this is uggarded system, GRPL is not activated. From technical perspective, if you have custom field in ACDOCA which could identify such of adjustment posting, GRPL substitution rule could exclude them from releasing into ACDOCU.
BTW, since it was EC-CS, please make sure the transition / migration to GR process is following SAP note 2833748 - SAP S/4HANA OP ( "on premise" ) - Transition from EC-CS to SAP S/4HANA for Group Reporting. Otherwise the whole GR implementation might NOT be supported by SAP, even though you think GR is a greenfield implementation.

entry.title: Re: All leaf elements must be used as part of the associa...
entry.content:
Hi Nadine, 

Late response --> I was busy, project GL. 

The magic happens in the Node assigment in the Hierarchy with Directory view. Also you need to compound your Master Data View and assoc the MD view to your model-view. The error message is very simple, as everything at the end of the road :). You use a field which you do not link in the assoc. 

1. I always flatten my hierarchy so I can use the parent/parents and child/childs (have them in one row) --> (2_GV_CS_ITEM_HIERWITHDIRECTORY_01. This makes it more straight forward to understand the logic. Even if you would get your case running, what will the hierarchy use as parent? You now use the same ID as child and parent. This will not work. 

2. Then create a new MD view, with compounded key where you assoc the Hierarchy with Directory. Use this MD view in your fact / model-view and that is it. As always, just a process to understand, but it is very straight forward :). 

Check the screen shots below. I did simulate your case :). Working properly

 

Result: 

Raw data: 

 

Hierarchy data: 

RSHIEDIR like: 

 

Now what you need to do:

1. flatten. You need to create the Parent ID. 

SELECT H1."HEID",
H1."NODEID",
H1."CS_CHART" AS "CS_CHART_CH",
H1."CS_ITEM" AS "CS_ITME_CH",
H1."NODENAME" AS "NODENAME_CH",
H2."CS_CHART" AS "CS_CHART_P",
H2."CS_ITEM" AS "CS_ITME_P",
H2."NODENAME" AS "NODENAME_P",
H2."NODEID" AS "NODEID_P",
H1."IOBJNM"
FROM ... AS H1
LEFT OUTER JOIN ... AS H2 ON H1."HEID" = H2."HEID" AND H1."PARENTID" = H2."NODEID" 

-- do not forget to use the "OBJVERS" = 'A' in BW 🙂

2. properly assign the parents and children fields

 

 

 

3. Create a MD view and use compounded key

Associate the Hierarchy with Directory

 

 

4. use the MD-view in a model-view/FACT. Create model on top. 

 

This took more then a couple of minutes, which I could use to play with my kids f.e. :), so please do kudo and accept the solution. Thanks, Martin

entry.title: Unsupported media type 415 error when using cap-js/attachment via HTTP POST
entry.content:
I'm trying to upload an attachment via HTTP POST OData call to my CAP service entity that has an extended Attachments field/association from cap-js/attachments via a React external app, but I'm encountering a 415 HTTP error code. I can successfully create an entry to my main entity, but I'm unable to create an attachment due to the 415 error. Has anyone encountered this?

So far the terminal logs isn't showing any error, only in the browser.

entry.title: Re: Unsupported media type 415 error when using cap-js/at...
entry.content: Nevermind, figured it out myself. I tried enabling draft on the entity to try creating the attachment via fiori element. I have observed the network tab and the http headers used, it uses multipart/mixed and application/json. I have been passing parameters as multipart/form-data from the react external app, hence receiving 415 http error.

entry.title: Re: DBVM not updated after data migration
entry.content:
Hi

After the system conversion, you should run report PPH_SETUP_MRPRECORDS to
populate the new planning file table with operative MRP records (PP-MRP) and the report
PPH_SETUP_MRPRECORDS_SIMU for simulative MRP records (PP-MP-LTP). Alternatively, you can also use the
conversion reports PPH_CONVERT_MRPRECORDS and PPH_CONVERT_MRPRECORDS_SIMU

entry.title: Re: Intercompany transactions
entry.content:
Hi @nagakumari ,

You cannot use substitution here as you cannot change the posting keys of lines getting generate. The only custom way to do this is via BADI. Use badi such as badi_acc_document, try to first identify your doc type, company code, and exact business transaction you want to modify, and then modify/replace the GL accounts, D/C indicator, posting keys.etc to meet your expectations.

I hope this answer your questions.

Thanks,

Divyesh

entry.title: Why Is My Purchase Order (PO) Delayed? Using OpenAI to Make SAP Actually Talk
entry.content:
Why Is My PO Delayed? Using OpenAI to Make SAP Actually Talk

Let me guess—you’re in SAP ME23N, trying to understand why that PO is still open. You’ve clicked through MD04, peeked at the long text, scanned the vendor confirmation, maybe even shot off a panicked email.

And still… no clarity.

Here’s the thing: SAP gives you data. AI gives you the explanation.

The Question That Sparked This Post

One of our clients asked me:

“Can’t SAP just tell me why the PO is delayed—in normal language?”

And that stopped me. Because… why not?

If we can ask ChatGPT to explain black holes, why can’t SAP just tell us why PO450001 is delayed?
And actually sound like a human?

Turns out—it can.

From Clues to Clarity: How OpenAI Helps SAP Make Sense

Every delayed PO leaves behind a breadcrumb trail:

  • MD04 long text says “QC not cleared”

  • MSEG has no GR date

  • Vendor note hints at shipment damage

  • MD06 shows exception 30

Now, if you’re fluent in SAPese, great. But for everyone else? AI can translate this noise into clarity.

Imagine this flow:

  1. User types: “Why is PO450001 delayed?”

  2. ABAP gathers delivery schedules, MRP messages, vendor texts, MD04 notes.

  3. Middleware (Python or Node.js) sends it to OpenAI.

  4. GPT reads the context and responds:

    “Vendor has not dispatched the goods. QC clearance is pending. MD04 indicates no upcoming GR. Recommend follow-up with QA and vendor.”

No digging. No hunting across 5 transactions. Just clarity.

What This Actually Looks Like (No Code, Just Logic)

Here’s the high-level blueprint:

SAP → Middleware → OpenAI → Human Explanation

  • ABAP or Fiori app assembles the facts.

  • Middleware sends the structured prompt to OpenAI.

  • GPT responds in plain English, summarizing reasoning.

  • You display this in a chatbot panel, SAP Card, or email.

Suddenly, SAP starts feeling like a teammate, not a maze.

Okay, But Why Not Just Use a Custom Report?

You can. And in many cases, you should.

But here’s what we’ve seen across different roles:

Role Typical Feedback

Sales“I see the PO, but why is it late?”
Procurement Head“I don’t want to read every MRP line.”
New Planner“I have no idea what these exceptions mean.”

OpenAI bridges the gap between technical depth and functional understanding.
It’s not just report output—it’s meaningful delivery.

 How This Plays With SAP Joule & SAP AI

You might be thinking:

“Wait—doesn’t SAP have Joule for this now?”

Yes—and it’s powerful. Here’s how to choose your path:

🟦 Prefer Native? SAP Joule Might Be Your Best Bet

If you're on S/4HANA Cloud or SuccessFactors, SAP Joule is a built-in assistant that understands SAP metadata, honors user roles, and works out-of-the-box—no middleware required. It’s your AI assistant with enterprise-grade security baked in.

🧠 Building Your Own SAP-Savvy LLM?

If you're training your own model on SAP notes, planner comments, and connected systems (PLM, MES, JIRA, etc.), this blog could be of use. Replace the OpenAI call with your internal LLM endpoint—the SAP-to-AI flow stays the same.

Whether you want agility or control, this approach scales both ways.

Want to Try This Yourself?

Here’s a simple Proof of Concept to play with:

  • Create a Fiori tile: “Explain PO Delay”

  • ABAP collects long texts, MD04 exceptions, vendor comments

  • Middleware sends a prompt like:

    “PO450001 is not GR’d. MD04 shows QC hold. Vendor note says raw material shortage.”

  • GPT responds:

    “PO is delayed due to raw material shortage at vendor and pending QC clearance. MD04 confirms no GR expected. Suggest escalation to QA team.”

✅It reads your SAP data like a planner
✅Replies like a colleague

 

Next Blog Sneak Peek 👀

Coming up next:

🔧How to auto-generate visual work instructions from SAP PM orders using OpenAI’s image generation API (DALL·E).

entry.title: Re: Automatic clearing of Incoming payments with Structur...
entry.content:
Hi @mshl ,

This is a common problem when bank cannot provide separate entries. Check with your Bank if they offer CAMT.054 format instead of CAMT.053. The version 054 what i know it comes with detailed information (per transaction).

If that doesn't work, then try to go wtih BADi route doing a custom enhancement whereby loop TxDtls in the XML, then generate multiple posting lines from single entry and assign structured references (like invoice reference number) to clearing logic. This should 100% work. Badi's you can search online which one to use.

I hope this will answer your question.

Thanks,

Divyesh
